title: FIX Simulator Pro - Trading Overview
description: End-to-end monitoring for the FIX Protocol Simulator pipeline

panels.title: FIX Messages / sec by Type
panels.description:
Rate of incoming trading messages per second, broken down by type.

- **new_order** – a trader is submitting a buy or sell instruction
- **logon** – a trading client has connected to the system
- **heartbeat** – a keep-alive ping confirming the connection is still live

Spikes in new_order indicate bursts of trading activity. Zero values mean no clients are currently connected.

panels.title: Active FIX Sessions
panels.description:
Number of trading clients currently connected to the gateway.

In a real exchange each session represents a broker, market-maker, or algorithmic trading system. The colour threshold is illustrative:
- **Green** – normal load
- **Yellow** – 10+ concurrent sessions
- **Red** – 50+ sessions (high load)

The counter increments on every logon message received and does not decrement automatically — it reflects the total number of logons seen since the system started.

panels.title: Parse Errors / min
panels.description:
Number of messages per minute that the gateway could not understand.

A message fails to parse when it is malformed — missing required fields, wrong format, or corrupted data. Every such message is safely quarantined to a dead-letter queue so the rest of the pipeline is never blocked.

**Ideal value: 0.** Any non-zero reading means a client sent a badly formatted order that was rejected before it could cause harm.

panels.title: Message Type Distribution (5 min window)
panels.description:
Share of each message type received in the last 5 minutes.

Shows what kind of activity is happening right now:
- A large **new_order** slice means traders are actively placing orders
- A large **heartbeat** slice means sessions are connected but quiet
- A **logon** slice appears when new clients connect

Useful for quickly seeing whether the system is processing real trade flow or is simply idle with connected clients.

panels.title: Trades Executed / sec
panels.description:
Rate of successful trade matches per second, broken down by trading instrument (symbol).

A trade is created when a buy order and a sell order for the same instrument meet at a matching price. Each line represents one instrument (e.g. EURUSD, AAPL, BTCUSD).

**Rising lines** = active trading. **Flat at zero** = no crossing orders in the book right now.

panels.title: Matching Latency P99
panels.description:
The time taken to match the slowest 1% of orders.

99% of all trades complete faster than this value. Think of it as the "worst case you'll see in normal operation."

- **Green (< 10 ms)** – excellent, sub-millisecond exchange-grade performance
- **Yellow (10–50 ms)** – acceptable under load
- **Red (> 50 ms)** – the engine is struggling; investigate queue depth or CPU

panels.title: Matching Latency P50
panels.description:
The typical (median) time to match an order — half of all trades complete faster than this value.

This is the number most traders would experience on an average interaction. A low, stable median combined with a low P99 (see the gauge to the left) means consistently fast performance with no hidden outliers.

- **Green (< 5 ms)** – excellent
- **Yellow (5–20 ms)** – acceptable
- **Red (> 20 ms)** – degraded; the matching engine may be overloaded

panels.title: Order Book Depth (resting orders)
panels.description:
Number of unmatched buy (green) and sell (red) orders waiting in the queue for each instrument.

Think of the order book as a marketplace notice board: buy orders on one side, sell orders on the other. When a buyer and seller agree on a price, their orders are removed and a trade is created.

**Balanced depth** (similar buy and sell counts) indicates a healthy, liquid market. **One-sided depth** means orders on that side are not finding counterparts — possibly because prices are too far apart.

panels.title: Cumulative Trades Matched by Symbol
panels.description:
Running total of all successfully matched trades since the system started, per instrument.

This is an ever-increasing counter — it never resets. A steadily rising line confirms the full pipeline (gateway → risk check → matching engine → trade store) is working correctly end to end.

Pair this with the Order Book Depth panel: if buy/sell queues are filling up but this counter stays flat, orders are arriving but not crossing — likely a price mismatch between buyers and sellers.

panels.title: API Request Rate
panels.description:
How frequently the Trade Store REST API is being called, broken down by HTTP method, endpoint, and response code.

The Trade Store exposes completed trades via a REST API (e.g. GET /trades). External systems — dashboards, reporting tools, downstream consumers — query it here.

**Green 2xx codes** = successful responses. **Red 5xx codes** = server errors that need attention. A sustained rise in request rate can indicate high downstream demand or a monitoring loop running frequently.

panels.title: API Latency P99
panels.description:
How long the slowest 1% of Trade Store API requests take to respond.

99% of all API calls complete faster than this. It is the "ceiling" a downstream consumer can expect under real conditions.

- **Green (< 100 ms)** – excellent for a database-backed REST API
- **Yellow (100–500 ms)** – acceptable, but worth monitoring
- **Red (> 500 ms)** – the database or network may be under stress; users will notice slow responses

panels.title: 5xx Error Rate / min
panels.description:
Number of internal server errors returned by the Trade Store API per minute.

HTTP 5xx responses mean the server encountered an unexpected problem — a database failure, a crashed service, or an unhandled exception. These are distinct from 4xx client errors (bad requests from callers).

**Ideal value: 0.** Any reading above zero means something failed on the server side and should be investigated immediately.

panels.title: API Latency Percentiles (p50 / p95 / p99)
panels.description:
Three latency percentiles shown together over time, giving a full picture of API response speed:

- **p50** – the typical experience: half of all requests are faster than this
- **p95** – the near-worst case: 95% of requests are faster than this
- **p99** – the ceiling: only 1 in 100 requests is slower

If p50 is low but p99 is high, there are occasional slow outliers worth investigating. If all three rise together, the system is under general load.

panels.title: Kafka Messages Consumed / sec
panels.description:
How fast each microservice is reading messages off the internal message queue (Kafka), broken down by topic and service.

Kafka is the backbone connecting all services: orders flow from the gateway through risk checks to the matching engine via these topics. Each service reads from the topic relevant to its role.

**A drop in this rate** means a service has slowed down, restarted, or stopped processing — messages will pile up in the queue and trades will be delayed.

panels.title: Exec Reports / sec by Type
panels.description:
Rate of FIX Execution Reports (35=8) emitted per second, broken down by ExecType and originating service.

- **0 (risk-service)** – New: order accepted by the risk check; confirmation wired back to the client
- **8 (risk-service)** – Rejected: order blocked by a risk rule before reaching the matching engine
- **F (matching-engine)** – Fill: a trade matched; one report is emitted per side (buy *and* sell), so this rate is 2× the trade rate

If all three lines are flat at zero, exec report emission has stopped — check for Kafka connectivity issues or look at the Failed Exec Report Emissions panel in the Loki dashboard.

panels.title: Fill Reports vs Trades (2:1 Ratio Check)
panels.description:
Structural sanity check: every trade produces exactly two Fill exec reports — one for the buyer, one for the seller. The blue line (Fill reports) should track the orange line (2 × trade rate) exactly.

**Divergence signals a bug:** if Fill reports fall below 2× trades, some exec report sends are silently failing (check the Loki 'Failed Exec Report Emissions' panel). If they exceed 2×, duplicate reports are being emitted.

panels.title: Risk Decisions: Approved vs Rejected (FIX view)
panels.description:
Approved (ExecType=0) and Rejected (ExecType=8) exec reports from the risk-service, plotted as rates. This is the FIX Protocol view of the same decision the risk-service makes — useful for confirming that approvals and rejections are being wired to clients.

A rising Rejected line with a stable Approved line can indicate a specific risk rule is firing frequently — cross-reference with the Loki 'Rejected Orders Only' panel to find the `reason` field.

panels.title: Kafka Messages Produced / sec
panels.description:
How fast messages are being written to the internal message queue (Kafka), broken down by topic and service.

This should closely mirror the consumed rate (panel to the left). A healthy system produces and consumes at roughly the same pace — messages flow straight through.

**If produced rate exceeds consumed rate**, messages are accumulating in the queue — a downstream service may be overwhelmed or stopped. This is an early warning sign of a processing backlog.
